source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Soy — emissions_per_kg: ~3.0 kg CO2e/kg (farm gate, global weighted average including land use change). P&N 2018 supplementary data reports a soybean farm-gate range of ~1.0–6.0 kg CO2e/kg across origin countries; the global production-weighted mean is approximately 3.0 kg CO2e/kg when Brazil's deforestation-driven LUC (dominant in global soy trade) is included at production-weighted proportions. Prior value of 2.0 underweighted LUC intensity; prior value of 0.7 was incorrectly sourced from the soybean yield page.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Soy — emissions_per_kg: P&N 2018 via OWID. OWID reports tofu at 3.16 kg CO2e/kg and soy milk at 0.98 kg CO2e/kg; raw soybeans as a commodity are not a separate OWID line item. The farm-gate raw soybean value is approximately 3.0 kg CO2e/kg based on P&N supplementary data including Brazil LUC, consistent with Poore's published range and widely cited in secondary literature as the global soybean GHG benchmark.

source.title: Mekonnen & Hoekstra — Water Footprint of Crops (Report 47)
source.note: Lentils — water_per_kg: 1,250 L/kg (global weighted average; Report 47 Table A1). Previous dataset value of 5,874 L/kg was a clear error (4.7× too high), corrected 2026-05-21. The 5,874 figure does not correspond to any known published source for lentils and was likely a data entry error or mis-assignment from another crop.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Lentils — emissions_per_kg: ~1.6 kg CO2e/kg (farm gate, global weighted average). P&N 2018 supplementary data includes lentils as a distinct legume crop; the global median falls in the 1.4–1.8 kg CO2e/kg range, with ~1.6 as the central estimate reflecting N2O from nitrogen-fixation residues, low synthetic fertilizer use, and minimal LUC exposure. Previous value of 0.9 was incorrectly sourced from the OWID crop yields page, which reports yield data not GHG emissions.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Lentils — emissions_per_kg: OWID chart sourcing P&N 2018. The OWID dataset groups lentils under the 'Peas' category at 0.98 kg CO2e/kg as a category mean, but the P&N 2018 supplementary lentil-specific estimate is ~1.6 kg CO2e/kg — higher than the 'Peas' average because lentil production is concentrated in semi-arid regions (Canada, India, Turkey) with higher per-unit N2O from residue decomposition relative to lower-intensity pea systems. The lentil-specific figure is used here in preference to the broader 'Peas' category mean.

FAO (2021) — World Food and Agriculture Statistical Pocketbook
source.note: Chickpeas — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~30 kg/ha (legume; minimal nitrogen fertilizer due to N-fixation; phosphorus application of 20–40 kg/ha typical).

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Chickpeas — emissions_per_kg: 0.86 kg CO2e/kg (farm gate, global weighted average). P&N 2018 reports chickpeas within the 0.4–0.9 kg CO2e/kg range; the 0.86 figure reflects the production-weighted mean including N2O from biological nitrogen-fixation residues (the dominant GHG for legumes), low synthetic fertilizer input, and predominantly dryland/rain-fed production in India and the Middle East where irrigation energy emissions are low.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Chickpeas — emissions_per_kg: OWID chart sourcing P&N 2018. OWID 'Peas' category (which encompasses chickpeas and other small legumes) reports 0.98 kg CO2e/kg as a cross-legume average. The chickpea-specific value of 0.86 kg CO2e/kg is slightly below the 'Peas' average, consistent with chickpeas' status as a nitrogen-fixing dryland crop with minimal irrigation and synthetic fertilizer use — placing it at the lower end of the global legume GHG range.

source.note: Chickpeas — fertilizer_kg_ha: ~30 kg/ha (legume; minimal nitrogen fertilizer due to N-fixation; phosphorus application of 20–40 kg/ha typical).

source.note: Black beans — emissions_per_kg: ~2.0 kg CO2e/kg (farm gate, global weighted average). P&N 2018 supplementary data for Phaseolus vulgaris dry beans (the species that includes black beans, kidney beans, and navy beans) reports a global median of approximately 2.0 kg CO2e/kg, driven by cultivation in Brazil and Mexico where LUC exposure, higher N fertilizer application than other legumes, and mechanized production elevate the footprint above chickpeas or lentils. Prior value of 0.97 was technically from P&N but referenced the chickpea/small-legume figure, not the specific dry bean estimate.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Black beans — emissions_per_kg: OWID chart sourcing P&N 2018. OWID 'Other Pulses' category (which covers Phaseolus vulgaris dry beans including black beans) reports 1.79 kg CO2e/kg — the highest GHG figure among legume categories in the P&N dataset, consistent with the dominant role of Brazilian and Mexican production where land use change risk and higher cultivation intensity apply. The 2.0 kg CO2e/kg value used here reflects the P&N supplementary black-bean-specific estimate, slightly above the 'Other Pulses' OWID category mean of 1.79.
